name: Wingos Full Stack Software Engineer Interview
description: A focused technical interview pack for a full-stack role building an AI-powered educational product in a university research environment.

topics.name: Frontend Engineering with React and TypeScript
topics.description: Building maintainable, accessible, production-grade interfaces with React, TypeScript, and Next.js.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Frontend engineering with React and TypeScript is the practice of building the part of a web product that users see and interact with. React helps organize the interface into components, while TypeScript adds type checking so many mistakes are found before the code runs. Next.js can add routing, server rendering, static generation, and backend-for-frontend capabilities.

## What problem does it solve?

A growing interface can become difficult to change when components mix display logic, data fetching, state management, and business rules. Good frontend engineering keeps responsibilities clear, makes behavior easier to test, and allows teams to change one feature without breaking unrelated screens. It also helps the application remain fast, accessible, and understandable as more developers contribute.

## How it works

- Split features into focused components with clear inputs and outputs.
- Keep business rules outside purely visual components when possible.
- Use TypeScript types at boundaries, but validate data received from APIs at runtime.
- Choose client rendering, server rendering, or static generation according to data freshness, privacy, search visibility, and performance needs.
- Treat loading, empty, error, and permission states as normal parts of the design.
- Prefer predictable state ownership over storing the same state in several places.

## Common mistakes

- Creating large components that fetch data, apply business rules, and render the whole page.
- Assuming TypeScript proves that external API data is valid at runtime.
- Using global state for information that belongs to one component or page.
- Choosing a Next.js rendering mode without considering caching and user-specific data.

## Key terms

- component — a reusable unit of interface and behavior.
- props — values passed from a parent component to a child component.
- state — data that can change while the interface is running.
- hydration — attaching client-side React behavior to server-rendered HTML.
- runtime validation — checking real incoming data while the application runs.

topics.name: Backend and API Engineering
topics.description: Designing reliable services and APIs with Node.js or Python/FastAPI.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Backend and API engineering is the design of server-side software that applies business rules, protects data, and exposes operations to frontend or external clients. Node.js runs JavaScript on the server and is well suited to input/output-heavy work. FastAPI is a Python framework that supports typed request models and automatic API documentation.

## What problem does it solve?

A frontend should not directly control databases, permissions, or critical business rules. A backend creates a trusted boundary where requests are validated, users are authorized, operations are coordinated, and consistent responses are returned. A well-designed API also allows clients and services to evolve without depending on internal implementation details.

## How it works

- Define resources and operations around product behavior rather than database tables alone.
- Validate request shape, field rules, identity, and permissions before changing data.
- Return consistent status codes and structured error responses.
- Make retryable operations idempotent when duplicate requests could cause harm.
- Keep CPU-heavy work away from the Node.js event loop by using worker threads, background jobs, or a separate service.
- Choose Node.js or FastAPI based on team skills, libraries, workload, deployment needs, and long-term ownership.
- Version or evolve contracts in a backward-compatible way when clients cannot change together.

## Common mistakes

- Treating API design as only choosing endpoint names.
- Performing slow CPU work directly in a Node.js request handler.
- Returning internal stack traces or inconsistent error formats to clients.
- Choosing a language only because one developer prefers it.

## Key terms

- API contract — the agreed request and response behavior clients depend on.
- event loop — the mechanism Node.js uses to coordinate asynchronous work.
- idempotency — the property that repeating an operation does not create an extra effect.
- validation — checking whether input is complete, correctly shaped, and allowed.
- backward compatibility — allowing older clients to keep working after a change.

topics.name: Data Persistence and Database Design
topics.description: Choosing and operating relational or document databases for a growing product.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Data persistence is the practice of storing information so it remains available after a request or application process ends. Database design decides how that information is structured, related, queried, protected, and changed over time. Relational databases such as PostgreSQL organize data into tables and relationships, while document databases such as MongoDB store flexible document-shaped records.

## What problem does it solve?

Products need reliable records for users, courses, assignments, progress, and permissions. Poor database choices can cause duplicate or inconsistent data, slow screens, unsafe migrations, and difficult reporting. A suitable model and access pattern make common operations predictable while preserving correctness as data volume and product requirements grow.

## How it works

- Start from important business entities, relationships, consistency rules, and query patterns.
- Prefer a relational model when transactions, joins, and strong constraints are central.
- Consider a document model when records are naturally self-contained and their shape changes frequently.
- Add indexes for frequent filters, joins, and sorting, then confirm their value with query plans and real measurements.
- Use transactions when several writes must succeed or fail as one unit.
- Deploy schema changes in compatible stages: expand the schema, update applications, migrate data, and remove old fields later.
- Monitor connection usage, slow queries, storage growth, and replication or backup health.

## Common mistakes

- Selecting PostgreSQL or MongoDB from popularity rather than data and query needs.
- Adding many indexes without considering write cost and storage.
- Running a breaking migration before every application instance supports it.
- Optimizing a query without examining its execution plan.

## Key terms

- schema — the structure and rules used to organize stored data.
- index — an additional data structure that speeds selected lookups.
- transaction — a group of operations treated as one consistent unit.
- query plan — the database's chosen steps for executing a query.
- migration — a controlled change to database structure or stored data.

topics.name: Accessible and Multilingual Educational UX
topics.description: Designing inclusive interfaces for teachers and students in a multilingual classroom context.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Accessible and multilingual educational UX is the design of interfaces that people can understand and operate across different abilities, languages, ages, and levels of technical confidence. Accessibility supports users who rely on keyboards, screen readers, zoom, captions, or other assistive technology. Multilingual design treats language as part of the product structure rather than replacing text at the end.

## What problem does it solve?

Teachers and students may use the same product in very different ways and under time pressure. An interface that works only with a mouse, assumes perfect vision, or breaks when translated can exclude users and create classroom disruption. Clear and inclusive workflows reduce mistakes, support independent use, and make pilot feedback more meaningful because usability problems are not confused with feature problems.

## How it works

- Use semantic HTML so controls and page structure have meaning for browsers and assistive tools.
- Ensure every action is reachable by keyboard and that focus moves in a logical, visible order.
- Provide labels, instructions, error messages, and status updates that do not depend only on color.
- Store interface text outside components and support variable text length, plural rules, dates, numbers, and locale-specific formats.
- Design teacher and student workflows separately, then test them with representative users.
- Validate important journeys with automated checks and manual keyboard and screen-reader testing.

## Common mistakes

- Adding ARIA attributes instead of using the correct native HTML element.
- Treating translation as a simple word-for-word text replacement.
- Designing only for the teacher's desktop and ignoring student devices.
- Assuming an automated accessibility score proves the whole workflow is usable.

## Key terms

- semantic HTML — elements whose names describe their purpose and structure.
- focus — the interface element currently receiving keyboard input.
- screen reader — software that presents interface content through speech or braille.
- internationalization — preparing software to support multiple languages and locales.
- localization — adapting content and formats for a particular language or region.

topics.name: LLM Service Integration
topics.description: Integrating LLM-based capabilities safely, reliably, and only where they add product value.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

LLM service integration means adding a large language model to a product through an API or hosted service. The model can generate, classify, summarize, or transform text, but its output is probabilistic rather than guaranteed. A useful integration surrounds the model with product rules, validation, monitoring, and a safe fallback experience.

## What problem does it solve?

Some educational tasks involve open-ended language where fixed rules are expensive or too rigid, such as drafting explanations or adapting text to a learner. An LLM can provide value in these cases, but it can also produce inaccurate answers, expose sensitive data, become slow, or create unpredictable cost. Integration engineering turns a model demonstration into a feature that can be trusted within defined limits.

## How it works

- Begin with a measurable user problem and compare the LLM approach with a deterministic alternative.
- Define what data may be sent, what output is allowed, and when human review is required.
- Use structured prompts and, where suitable, request structured output that can be validated.
- Ground answers in approved source material when factual accuracy matters.
- Test with a representative evaluation set, including difficult and unsafe inputs.
- Control latency and cost through smaller models, caching, batching, shorter context, timeouts, and asynchronous workflows.
- Record model version, request metadata, quality signals, failures, and user feedback without logging sensitive content unnecessarily.

## Common mistakes

- Using an LLM because it is fashionable rather than because it improves a user outcome.
- Treating confident language as proof that an answer is correct.
- Sending complete user records when only a small data subset is needed.
- Testing only a few successful examples before release.

## Key terms

- prompt — instructions and context sent to the model.
- grounding — connecting generation to trusted source information.
- hallucination — plausible-looking output that is unsupported or false.
- evaluation set — a repeatable collection of examples used to measure quality.
- token — a unit of text used for model input, output, cost, and limits.

topics.name: Web Security and Privacy
topics.description: Protecting users, data, APIs, secrets, and AI-assisted workflows.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Web security and privacy are the practices used to prevent unauthorized access, harmful actions, data exposure, and misuse of personal information. Security protects systems and users from threats, while privacy controls why personal data is collected, how much is used, where it is sent, and how long it is kept. Both must be designed across the frontend, API, database, infrastructure, and external services.

## What problem does it solve?

An educational product may hold identities, classroom membership, assignments, and student activity. A small authorization mistake can reveal one student's information to another user, while leaked secrets can expose the whole platform. External LLM services add another data boundary. Security and privacy controls reduce these risks and make product behavior easier to audit and explain.

## How it works

- Authenticate the user, then authorize every protected action on the server using roles, ownership, and resource context.
- Validate and encode untrusted input to reduce injection and cross-site scripting risks.
- Protect sessions and tokens with secure storage, expiration, rotation, and appropriate cookie settings.
- Keep secrets outside source code and restrict service permissions to the minimum required.
- Encrypt network traffic and protect sensitive stored data and backups.
- Minimize personal data, define retention rules, and avoid sending unnecessary student information to third parties.
- Log security-relevant events without recording passwords, tokens, or sensitive content.

## Common mistakes

- Hiding a button in the frontend and calling that authorization.
- Trusting identifiers supplied by the client without checking resource ownership.
- Logging complete requests that contain tokens or personal data.
- Sending raw student content to an LLM before reviewing provider and retention settings.

## Key terms

- authentication — proving who a user or service is.
- authorization — deciding what an authenticated identity may do.
- least privilege — granting only the permissions needed for a task.
- data minimization — collecting and sharing only necessary personal data.
- threat model — a structured view of valuable assets, possible attackers, and attack paths.

topics.name: Testing and Software Quality
topics.description: Testing a full-stack product from isolated logic to critical user journeys.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Testing and software quality are the practices used to check that a product behaves correctly, remains maintainable, and protects important user journeys as it changes. Different test levels provide different kinds of confidence. Unit tests check focused logic, integration tests check connected parts, contract tests check service boundaries, and end-to-end tests exercise the product through a real user flow.

## What problem does it solve?

A full-stack product can fail even when each isolated function appears correct. An API contract may change, permissions may be applied incorrectly, or a browser workflow may break after a deployment. Testing creates repeatable evidence about expected behavior and turns past defects into protection against regression. It also supports faster delivery because developers can change code with clearer feedback.

## How it works

- Identify the most important business rules, security boundaries, and user journeys.
- Test pure logic with fast unit tests and real component interactions with focused integration tests.
- Use contract tests to detect disagreement between frontend, backend, and external services.
- Keep a small set of stable end-to-end tests for critical journeys such as login, assignment access, and saving work.
- For LLM features, evaluate properties and quality thresholds over a fixed dataset rather than expecting one exact sentence.
- When fixing a production bug, reproduce it with a failing test at the lowest useful level before changing the code.
- Run suitable checks in CI and track flaky tests instead of repeatedly retrying them without investigation.

## Common mistakes

- Testing implementation details that change during harmless refactoring.
- Relying only on slow end-to-end tests.
- Mocking every dependency so the tested system no longer resembles production.
- Expecting non-deterministic LLM output to match one exact string.

## Key terms

- regression — a previously working behavior that breaks after a change.
- test double — a controlled replacement for a real dependency in a test.
- contract test — a check that two systems agree on their interface.
- flaky test — a test that passes or fails without a relevant code change.
- evaluation metric — a defined measurement used to judge output quality.

topics.name: CI/CD, Docker, and Delivery Automation
topics.description: Automating builds, validation, deployment, rollback, and environment management.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

CI/CD, Docker, and delivery automation are practices that move a code change through building, validation, packaging, deployment, and rollback in a repeatable way. Continuous integration checks every change against shared quality rules. Continuous delivery keeps the software in a releasable state, while Docker packages an application with a defined runtime environment.

## What problem does it solve?

Manual releases are slow, inconsistent, and difficult to audit. A change may work on one developer's machine but fail because production has different dependencies or configuration. Automated delivery provides quick feedback, creates the same artifact for each environment, reduces human error, and makes small, reversible releases practical.

## How it works

- On each change, install locked dependencies and run formatting, linting, type checks, tests, and security scans.
- Build a versioned application artifact or container image once, then promote the same artifact between environments.
- Use multi-stage Docker builds, a small trusted base image, a non-root user, and explicit runtime configuration.
- Keep secrets in a secure secret store rather than inside images or repository files.
- Apply database changes with a backward-compatible migration strategy.
- Deploy through rolling, blue-green, or canary methods according to risk and platform support.
- Verify health and key metrics after release, and provide an automated or well-tested rollback path.
- Use feature flags to separate code deployment from user exposure when useful.

## Common mistakes

- Rebuilding different artifacts for test and production.
- Copying development tools and secrets into the final Docker image.
- Treating a successful deployment command as proof that the release is healthy.
- Depending on rollback while deploying an irreversible database change.

## Key terms

- pipeline — the automated sequence that validates and delivers a change.
- artifact — a versioned output such as a package or container image.
- canary release — exposing a change to a small portion of traffic first.
- feature flag — a runtime switch controlling whether users see a feature.
- rollback — returning to a previously known working release.

topics.name: Reliability, Performance, and Observability
topics.description: Operating and troubleshooting a serious web product with measurable service health.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Reliability, performance, and observability describe how a product behaves in production and how clearly the team can understand that behavior. Reliability is the ability to provide correct service over time. Performance concerns response time and resource use. Observability uses logs, metrics, traces, and user-facing signals to explain what is happening inside a distributed system.

## What problem does it solve?

A system can appear healthy at the server level while teachers experience slow saves or failed classroom workflows. Without useful telemetry, teams guess at causes and cannot tell whether a release improved the situation. Reliability engineering defines acceptable service, detects problems early, and provides enough evidence to investigate failures across browser, API, database, and external services.

## How it works

- Define service-level indicators such as successful request rate, latency, and critical journey completion.
- Set service-level objectives that describe an acceptable target over a period of time.
- Collect structured logs with correlation identifiers, numerical metrics, and distributed traces across important calls.
- Monitor technical signals together with product signals, such as assignment saves completed successfully.
- Alert on user impact and sustained objective violations rather than every small resource change.
- Investigate incidents by narrowing the time window, affected users, recent releases, dependencies, and slow trace segments.
- Handle classroom traffic peaks with capacity planning, caching, queueing, autoscaling, and controlled degradation where appropriate.
- Use post-incident reviews to improve code, monitoring, runbooks, and architecture.

## Common mistakes

- Collecting large amounts of logs without a question they can answer.
- Monitoring only CPU and memory while ignoring failed user journeys.
- Adding retries without timeouts, limits, or protection against duplicate work.
- Scaling application servers while leaving the database bottleneck unchanged.

## Key terms

- metric — a numerical measurement tracked over time.
- trace — a connected record of work across services and dependencies.
- SLI — a measurement that represents service behavior.
- SLO — a target value for an SLI over a defined period.
- graceful degradation — providing reduced functionality instead of complete failure.

topics.name: Research-to-Product Engineering
topics.description: Turning research ideas and school feedback into a maintainable, production-ready educational platform.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

Research-to-product engineering is the process of turning a promising research idea into software that real users can understand, trust, and depend on. It connects experiments and academic goals with product discovery, architecture, delivery, measurement, and ongoing operation. The result is not only a demonstration of an idea, but a maintainable feature with clear value and known limitations.

## What problem does it solve?

Research work often begins with uncertainty: the algorithm may be promising, but the target user, workflow, data, success measure, and operational cost may still be unclear. Building the full system immediately can waste time or create a fragile prototype that cannot be safely piloted. A staged product approach reduces uncertainty while preserving the ability to learn from schools and research results.

## How it works

- Clarify the user problem, research hypothesis, affected users, constraints, and evidence that would count as success.
- Map the idea into a small end-to-end workflow and identify assumptions that need early testing.
- Build a prototype or technical spike only where uncertainty is high, then create a production plan for data, APIs, security, accessibility, and operations.
- Separate experimental components behind stable interfaces so they can be replaced without rewriting the whole product.
- Release to a controlled pilot with consent, support, monitoring, and a clear fallback.
- Gather qualitative feedback and quantitative product and research measures.
- Decide whether to iterate, scale, pause, or remove the feature based on evidence.
- Document decisions, limitations, and reproducibility details for engineers and researchers.

## Common mistakes

- Treating a successful research demo as production-ready software.
- Building a broad platform before validating the smallest useful workflow.
- Measuring model accuracy while ignoring teacher and student outcomes.
- Allowing temporary prototype shortcuts to become permanent architecture without review.

## Key terms

- hypothesis — a testable statement about expected behavior or value.
- prototype — a limited implementation built mainly to learn.
- pilot — a controlled real-world trial with selected users.
- success metric — a defined measurement used to judge whether the feature helps.
- technical spike — a short investigation used to reduce engineering uncertainty.

questions.title: Designing a maintainable React feature
questions.prompt: How would you structure a new React feature so that it remains easy to test, change, and reuse as the product grows?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain component boundaries, state ownership, data access, and testing.

expectedPoints.label: Defines clear component boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Separates presentation, domain logic, and data access based on responsibility rather than file size.

expectedPoints.label: Places state deliberately
expectedPoints.description: Keeps state close to where it is used and distinguishes local, server, and shared state.

expectedPoints.label: Designs for testability
expectedPoints.description: Uses stable interfaces and tests behavior at appropriate boundaries.

followUps.prompt: When would you introduce a shared state library instead of keeping state local?
How would you prevent a reusable component from becoming too generic?

questions.title: TypeScript and API contracts
questions.prompt: How would you keep TypeScript frontend types aligned with a backend API without creating false confidence?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss compile-time types, runtime validation, and contract generation or testing.

expectedPoints.label: Distinguishes compile-time and runtime safety
expectedPoints.description: Explains that TypeScript types disappear at runtime and cannot validate external data.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a shared contract source
expectedPoints.description: Suggests OpenAPI, generated clients, shared schemas, or another controlled source of truth.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies contract compatibility
expectedPoints.description: Includes contract tests, integration tests, or backward-compatible API evolution.

followUps.prompt: Which runtime validation approach would you use at the frontend boundary, and why?
How would you roll out a breaking response-shape change safely?

questions.title: Choosing a Next.js rendering strategy
questions.prompt: For a teacher dashboard and a public information page, how would you choose between client rendering, server rendering, and static generation?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare data freshness, personalization, SEO, latency, and operational complexity.

expectedPoints.label: Matches rendering to page needs
expectedPoints.description: Chooses based on personalization, freshness, interactivity, and public discoverability.

expectedPoints.label: Explains static and server rendering
expectedPoints.description: Recognizes static generation for stable public content and server rendering for request-time data.

expectedPoints.label: Explains client-rendering trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Mentions hydration, browser data fetching, perceived latency, and bundle cost.

followUps.prompt: Which parts of the teacher dashboard would you keep as server components and which as client components?
How would you prevent cached educational data from becoming stale?

questions.title: Node.js concurrency and the event loop
questions.prompt: A Node.js API becomes slow when one endpoint performs CPU-heavy document processing. Why can this affect unrelated requests, and how would you fix it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Explain the event loop, identify CPU blocking, and propose isolation or offloading.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies event-loop blocking
expectedPoints.description: Explains that CPU-heavy synchronous work blocks the event loop and delays other requests.

expectedPoints.label: Offloads CPU-heavy work
expectedPoints.description: Proposes worker threads, background jobs, a separate service, or an external processing system.

expectedPoints.label: Measures and protects the service
expectedPoints.description: Uses profiling, timeouts, concurrency limits, and monitoring before and after the change.

followUps.prompt: When would you choose worker threads over a separate background service?
How would you measure event-loop lag in production?

questions.title: Designing a production REST API
questions.prompt: How would you design an API endpoint that lets a teacher create and later update a learning activity?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover resources, validation, authorization, errors, concurrency, and API evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Uses clear resource and HTTP semantics
expectedPoints.description: Models the activity as a resource and uses appropriate methods, status codes, and identifiers.

expectedPoints.label: Validates and authorizes changes
expectedPoints.description: Validates input and checks that the teacher is allowed to modify the target resource.

expectedPoints.label: Handles concurrency and evolution
expectedPoints.description: Mentions optimistic concurrency, idempotency where relevant, and backward-compatible changes.

followUps.prompt: What should happen if two teachers or browser tabs edit the same activity at the same time?
What information should a validation error response contain?

questions.title: Node.js versus FastAPI for a service
questions.prompt: A new service could be built with Node.js or Python FastAPI. How would you decide rather than choosing only from personal preference?
questions.answerStructureHint: Compare workload, libraries, team skills, integration needs, and operational consistency.

expectedPoints.label: Considers workload and ecosystem
expectedPoints.description: Looks at I/O versus CPU or ML work and the libraries needed by the service.

expectedPoints.label: Considers team and operations
expectedPoints.description: Includes maintainability, team capability, deployment, monitoring, and consistency with existing services.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps service boundaries justified
expectedPoints.description: Avoids introducing another runtime unless the service boundary and benefits justify the added complexity.

followUps.prompt: Would an LLM integration automatically justify choosing Python? Why or why not?
What operational costs appear when a small team supports both Node.js and Python?

questions.title: PostgreSQL versus MongoDB
questions.prompt: For courses, users, assignments, and progress records, how would you decide between PostgreSQL and MongoDB?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss relationships, constraints, transactions, access patterns, and schema evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Starts from the data model and access patterns
expectedPoints.description: Evaluates relationships, query patterns, document boundaries, and expected changes.

expectedPoints.label: Explains relational strengths
expectedPoints.description: Mentions joins, constraints, transactions, and consistency for connected educational records.

expectedPoints.label: Explains document-store strengths
expectedPoints.description: Recognizes flexible nested aggregates and simple document-oriented access, while noting consistency trade-offs.

followUps.prompt: Which database would you initially choose for student progress, and what assumptions drive that choice?
When would using both databases be justified rather than unnecessary complexity?

questions.title: Diagnosing a slow database query
questions.prompt: A teacher dashboard query becomes slow as the number of students and activity records grows. How would you investigate and improve it?
questions.answerStructureHint: Measure the query, inspect the plan, improve indexing or shape, and verify the result.

expectedPoints.label: Inspects evidence before changing the database
expectedPoints.description: Uses query metrics and an execution plan to identify scans, joins, sorting, or row estimates.

expectedPoints.label: Improves indexes or query shape
expectedPoints.description: Designs indexes around filters, joins, and ordering, and avoids fetching unnecessary data.

expectedPoints.label: Verifies improvement and index costs
expectedPoints.description: Load-tests the change and considers write cost, storage, and maintenance overhead.

followUps.prompt: How does column order affect a composite index?
When would caching help, and when would it only hide the real problem?

questions.title: Safe database schema migration
questions.prompt: How would you deploy a schema change that must support both the old and new application versions during a rolling deployment?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe an expand-and-contract migration with compatibility, backfill, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Uses expand-and-contract
expectedPoints.description: Adds compatible schema first, migrates usage, then removes the old structure later.

expectedPoints.label: Handles existing data safely
expectedPoints.description: Plans backfill, dual reads or writes where needed, and idempotent migration execution.

expectedPoints.label: Observes and controls rollout
expectedPoints.description: Uses feature flags, validation, metrics, and a practical rollback or roll-forward plan.

followUps.prompt: How would you backfill a very large table without causing long locks?
Why can application rollback be harder after an irreversible data migration?

questions.title: Building an accessible learning interface
questions.prompt: What would you check when building an interactive learning interface that must be usable by keyboard and assistive-technology users?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover semantic structure, keyboard use, focus, names, feedback, and testing.

expectedPoints.label: Uses semantics and keyboard support
expectedPoints.description: Prefers native semantic elements and ensures every interaction works without a mouse.

expectedPoints.label: Manages focus and dynamic feedback
expectedPoints.description: Handles visible focus, modal focus, errors, and announcements for dynamic changes.

expectedPoints.label: Tests accessibility in multiple ways
expectedPoints.description: Combines automated checks with keyboard and screen-reader testing.

followUps.prompt: When is ARIA necessary, and why is native HTML usually preferable?
How would you make a form validation error understandable to a screen-reader user?

questions.title: Designing a multilingual interface
questions.prompt: What technical and UX concerns would you consider when building a product for Luxembourg's multilingual classrooms?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss internationalization architecture, content, layout, locale behavior, and testing.

expectedPoints.label: Externalizes and manages translatable content
expectedPoints.description: Keeps text out of components and uses stable message keys, translation workflows, and fallbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Handles locale-sensitive behavior
expectedPoints.description: Covers dates, numbers, pluralization, sorting, and possibly right-to-left layout.

expectedPoints.label: Separates interface language from learning content
expectedPoints.description: Recognizes that user UI language, course language, and generated content language may differ.

followUps.prompt: How would you keep LLM-generated content in the requested classroom language?
How would you test layouts for long translations and missing messages?

questions.title: Designing for teachers and students
questions.prompt: Teachers and students have different goals and levels of technical confidence. How would you design and validate workflows for both groups?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify distinct users, simplify workflows, prototype, observe, and iterate.

expectedPoints.label: Models distinct user needs
expectedPoints.description: Separates teacher and student jobs, permissions, context, and success criteria.

expectedPoints.label: Reduces cognitive load
expectedPoints.description: Uses clear language, sensible defaults, progressive disclosure, and recoverable actions.

expectedPoints.label: Validates with real users
expectedPoints.description: Uses prototypes, observation, usability tests, and product metrics rather than relying only on assumptions.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if teachers request flexibility that makes the student experience more confusing?
Which metrics would show that a classroom workflow is actually improving?

questions.title: Deciding where an LLM adds value
questions.prompt: How would you decide whether a proposed educational feature should use an LLM or a deterministic implementation?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define user value, compare alternatives, assess risk, and define measurable success.

expectedPoints.label: Starts from the user problem
expectedPoints.description: Defines the user need and avoids using an LLM only because it is available.

expectedPoints.label: Matches LLMs to suitable tasks
expectedPoints.description: Uses LLMs for probabilistic language tasks while preferring deterministic logic for strict rules or calculations.

expectedPoints.label: Defines evaluation and risk controls
expectedPoints.description: Considers quality, latency, cost, privacy, failure modes, and a baseline alternative.

followUps.prompt: Give an example where a rules-based solution would be better than an LLM.
What would a useful offline evaluation dataset contain?

questions.title: Making an LLM feature reliable
questions.prompt: An LLM generates learning explanations, but some answers are inaccurate or inconsistent. How would you improve the feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover grounding, constraints, evaluation, human control, and safe fallback behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Grounds and constrains generation
expectedPoints.description: Uses trusted context, structured outputs, clear instructions, and validation where possible.

expectedPoints.label: Builds a repeatable evaluation loop
expectedPoints.description: Uses representative test cases, error categories, automated checks, and human review.

expectedPoints.label: Uses fallbacks and transparency
expectedPoints.description: Handles low confidence or failures without presenting uncertain output as guaranteed truth.

followUps.prompt: What new failure modes does retrieval-augmented generation introduce?
Where would you place teacher review in the workflow without making the feature too slow?

questions.title: Controlling LLM latency and cost
questions.prompt: An LLM-assisted feature is useful but slow and expensive during school hours. How would you reduce latency and cost without destroying quality?
questions.answerStructureHint: Measure usage, reduce unnecessary work, choose models deliberately, cache safely, and degrade gracefully.

expectedPoints.label: Measures the real cost and latency drivers
expectedPoints.description: Breaks down model latency, token use, request frequency, retries, and peak patterns.

expectedPoints.label: Optimizes model and prompt usage
expectedPoints.description: Uses smaller models where sufficient, shorter context, batching, streaming, or asynchronous processing.

expectedPoints.label: Caches and degrades safely
expectedPoints.description: Caches reusable outputs with correct keys and freshness rules, and provides a non-LLM fallback when needed.

followUps.prompt: Which LLM outputs are safe to cache, and which should never be shared across users?
Does streaming reduce actual latency or only perceived latency?

questions.title: Authentication and authorization
questions.prompt: How would you design authentication and authorization for teachers, students, and administrators?
questions.answerStructureHint: Separate identity from permissions, define roles and resource checks, and secure sessions.

expectedPoints.label: Designs secure identity and sessions
expectedPoints.description: Uses a proven identity provider or secure session flow with protected credentials and expiry.

expectedPoints.label: Authorizes every sensitive operation
expectedPoints.description: Uses role and resource-level checks on the server rather than trusting the UI.

expectedPoints.label: Handles lifecycle and audit needs
expectedPoints.description: Considers account provisioning, revocation, school boundaries, and auditable sensitive actions.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent a teacher from accessing data belonging to another school?
When would you prefer server-side sessions over self-contained JWTs?

questions.title: Securing a full-stack web application
questions.prompt: What are the main security risks you would review before releasing a new teacher-facing feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover input, output, access control, browser threats, secrets, dependencies, and abuse.

expectedPoints.label: Protects input and output boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Validates input and prevents injection and unsafe HTML rendering.

expectedPoints.label: Covers access and browser security
expectedPoints.description: Checks authorization, CSRF where relevant, cookie flags, CORS, and security headers.

expectedPoints.label: Considers dependencies and abuse
expectedPoints.description: Includes secret management, dependency scanning, rate limiting, and audit logging.

followUps.prompt: When is CSRF relevant, and how does the authentication mechanism affect the answer?
What makes user-generated rich text especially risky?

questions.title: LLM security and student-data privacy
questions.prompt: What security and privacy risks appear when student or classroom data is sent to an external LLM service?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss data minimization, provider controls, prompt injection, isolation, retention, and monitoring.

expectedPoints.label: Minimizes and controls data exposure
expectedPoints.description: Avoids sending unnecessary personal data and understands provider retention and training policies.

expectedPoints.label: Handles untrusted instructions and content
expectedPoints.description: Recognizes prompt injection and prevents model output or retrieved content from directly authorizing actions.

expectedPoints.label: Isolates users and preserves auditability
expectedPoints.description: Prevents cross-user leakage, controls logs, and records relevant AI actions and configuration.

followUps.prompt: What questions would you ask an LLM provider before sending educational data?
How would you secure an LLM that can call internal application tools?

questions.title: Full-stack testing strategy
questions.prompt: How would you divide testing across unit, integration, contract, and end-to-end tests for this product?
questions.answerStructureHint: Assign each test type a purpose, prioritize risk, and keep the suite reliable.

expectedPoints.label: Gives each test layer a clear purpose
expectedPoints.description: Uses unit tests for isolated logic, integration tests for boundaries, and end-to-end tests for critical journeys.

expectedPoints.label: Prioritizes risk and behavior
expectedPoints.description: Tests important user outcomes, permissions, data integrity, and failure paths rather than implementation details.

expectedPoints.label: Keeps the suite fast and trustworthy
expectedPoints.description: Controls fixtures, avoids flaky dependencies, parallelizes safely, and tracks flaky tests.

followUps.prompt: Which three end-to-end journeys would you automate first?
When does mocking make a test less trustworthy?

questions.title: Testing an LLM-backed feature
questions.prompt: How would you test a feature whose LLM output is non-deterministic?
questions.answerStructureHint: Separate deterministic code, evaluate output qualities, control randomness, and monitor production.

expectedPoints.label: Separates deterministic and model-dependent logic
expectedPoints.description: Tests prompt construction, parsing, authorization, and fallback logic independently from live model quality.

expectedPoints.label: Uses evaluation criteria and datasets
expectedPoints.description: Evaluates representative examples with rubrics, thresholds, and human review where necessary.

expectedPoints.label: Controls variables and monitors drift
expectedPoints.description: Pins model and prompt versions, lowers randomness for tests, and watches production quality changes.

followUps.prompt: Would you call the real LLM provider in every CI run? Why or why not?
How would you evaluate whether an explanation is educationally useful, not merely fluent?

questions.title: Turning a production bug into lasting protection
questions.prompt: A bug allowed a student to see the wrong assignment. How would you fix it and prevent the same class of issue from returning?
questions.answerStructureHint: Contain the issue, find the root cause, add the right tests and controls, and verify affected data.

expectedPoints.label: Contains and assesses impact
expectedPoints.description: Stops exposure, identifies affected users or data, and preserves evidence.

expectedPoints.label: Finds the root cause and invariant
expectedPoints.description: Identifies the missing authorization or data-boundary rule rather than patching only one symptom.

expectedPoints.label: Adds layered regression protection
expectedPoints.description: Adds tests, server-side checks, monitoring, and possibly data validation or audit rules.

followUps.prompt: How would you determine whether the bug exposed historical data before it was discovered?
Which test layer should catch this kind of authorization failure?

questions.title: Designing a CI/CD pipeline
questions.prompt: What stages would you include in a CI/CD pipeline for a React, API, and database application?
questions.answerStructureHint: Walk from pull request validation to artifact creation, deployment, verification, and rollback.

expectedPoints.label: Validates changes before merge
expectedPoints.description: Includes linting, type checking, tests, security checks, and content or schema validation.

expectedPoints.label: Builds and promotes immutable artifacts
expectedPoints.description: Builds once, versions the artifact, and promotes the same artifact through environments.

expectedPoints.label: Deploys with verification and rollback
expectedPoints.description: Uses staged rollout, migrations, health checks, smoke tests, observability, and rollback or roll-forward.

followUps.prompt: How would the pipeline access deployment secrets without exposing them in logs?
What should happen automatically when post-deployment smoke tests fail?

questions.title: Production-ready Docker images
questions.prompt: What would you do to make a Docker image for a web API smaller, safer, and more predictable?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss multi-stage builds, pinned dependencies, non-root runtime, minimal contents, and health behavior.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a minimal reproducible build
expectedPoints.description: Uses multi-stage builds, lock files, pinned base images, and excludes development files.

expectedPoints.label: Hardens the runtime
expectedPoints.description: Runs as non-root, avoids embedded secrets, and reduces packages and privileges.

expectedPoints.label: Supports predictable operation
expectedPoints.description: Handles signals, graceful shutdown, health checks, configuration, and structured logs.

followUps.prompt: Why should secrets not be baked into the image, even in a private registry?
Why does graceful shutdown matter during a rolling deployment?

questions.title: Releasing a risky feature safely
questions.prompt: How would you release a new AI-assisted classroom feature when you are unsure how it will behave with real users?
questions.answerStructureHint: Describe controlled exposure, observability, fallback, feedback, and decision criteria.

expectedPoints.label: Limits initial exposure
expectedPoints.description: Uses feature flags, internal testing, a small pilot, or canary rollout.

expectedPoints.label: Defines technical and product feedback
expectedPoints.description: Tracks errors, latency, model quality, usage, and direct teacher or student feedback.

expectedPoints.label: Prepares fallback and stop criteria
expectedPoints.description: Can disable the feature quickly and defines thresholds for continuing, changing, or stopping the rollout.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent long-lived feature flags from becoming permanent technical debt?
What would you communicate to a pilot school before enabling the feature?

questions.title: Observability for a web product
questions.prompt: What would you monitor to understand whether the platform is healthy from both a technical and user perspective?
questions.answerStructureHint: Cover metrics, logs, traces, service-level indicators, and user journeys.

expectedPoints.label: Tracks core service signals
expectedPoints.description: Includes latency, traffic, errors, saturation, and dependency health.

expectedPoints.label: Uses correlated logs and traces
expectedPoints.description: Adds structured context and trace or request identifiers without leaking sensitive data.

expectedPoints.label: Measures user-visible outcomes
expectedPoints.description: Tracks critical workflows, frontend errors, availability, and suitable service-level objectives.

followUps.prompt: What SLI and SLO would you choose for opening a teacher dashboard?
How would you keep logs useful without storing sensitive student data?

questions.title: Debugging a cross-stack production incident
questions.prompt: Teachers report that saving an activity sometimes takes ten seconds, but the issue cannot be reproduced locally. How would you investigate?
questions.answerStructureHint: Form a timeline, trace the request across layers, compare affected cases, and test hypotheses.

expectedPoints.label: Scopes the incident with evidence
expectedPoints.description: Finds timing, affected users, frequency, recent changes, and correlation with load or dependencies.

expectedPoints.label: Traces the full request path
expectedPoints.description: Uses frontend timing, API spans, database queries, and external-service latency to isolate the delay.

expectedPoints.label: Tests and verifies hypotheses
expectedPoints.description: Compares slow and normal requests, reproduces under realistic conditions, and confirms the fix with metrics.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if distributed tracing had not been implemented yet?
How would your investigation change if saving synchronously called an LLM provider?

questions.title: Scaling for classroom traffic peaks
questions.prompt: The platform is quiet most of the day but receives sharp traffic peaks when classes begin. How would you design for this pattern?
questions.answerStructureHint: Discuss stateless scaling, caching, database protection, queues, capacity, and graceful degradation.

expectedPoints.label: Scales stateless application capacity
expectedPoints.description: Uses horizontal scaling, autoscaling or scheduled capacity, and avoids local session dependence.

expectedPoints.label: Protects shared dependencies
expectedPoints.description: Uses caching, connection limits, queues, rate limits, and backpressure to protect databases and external services.

expectedPoints.label: Plans degradation and validates capacity
expectedPoints.description: Defines non-critical features that can degrade and uses load tests based on realistic peaks.

followUps.prompt: How would you prevent LLM calls from amplifying a morning traffic spike?
Why can in-memory user sessions make horizontal scaling harder?

questions.title: Turning a research idea into a product feature
questions.prompt: A researcher proposes a promising adaptive-learning algorithm, but the product requirements are still unclear. How would you move from the idea to a production feature?
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the hypothesis, define a thin experiment, set success and safety criteria, then productize deliberately.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies hypothesis and user outcome
expectedPoints.description: Translates the research idea into a user problem, assumptions, and measurable educational or product outcomes.

expectedPoints.label: Builds a controlled thin experiment
expectedPoints.description: Creates a prototype or pilot with limited scope, representative data, and explicit evaluation criteria.

expectedPoints.label: Separates experiment from productionization
expectedPoints.description: Adds reliability, security, UX, maintainability, observability, and operational ownership before wider rollout.

followUps.prompt: What would you do if the technical prototype works but teachers do not find it useful?
How would you avoid optimizing only for a model metric that has little classroom value?

questions.title: Owning a feature from idea to production
questions.prompt: Tell me how you would approach owning a feature from initial discussion through release and ongoing maintenance.
questions.answerStructureHint: Use a concrete structure: clarify, design, deliver, validate, release, observe, and iterate.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies and designs before implementation
expectedPoints.description: Aligns users, scope, constraints, risks, data, and acceptance criteria before committing to a solution.

expectedPoints.label: Delivers collaboratively and incrementally
expectedPoints.description: Breaks work into reviewable increments and coordinates frontend, backend, QA, research, or product needs.

expectedPoints.label: Owns release and operation
expectedPoints.description: Plans rollout, monitoring, documentation, support, and later iteration based on evidence.

followUps.prompt: Give a concrete example of a feature where your original plan changed after feedback.
How do you decide what to postpone when time is limited?

questions.title: Designing the Wingos platform
questions.prompt: Design a high-level architecture for an AI-assisted educational platform used by teachers and students in multiple schools.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify requirements, define main components and data flows, then cover security, LLMs, reliability, and rollout.

expectedPoints.label: Clarifies requirements and boundaries
expectedPoints.description: Identifies users, core workflows, scale, school isolation, multilingual needs, and reliability expectations.

expectedPoints.label: Defines coherent components and data flows
expectedPoints.description: Includes frontend, API, identity, database, background processing, and external AI boundaries.

expectedPoints.label: Addresses quality attributes and evolution
expectedPoints.description: Covers security, privacy, observability, failure handling, deployment, and an incremental architecture.

followUps.prompt: What happens to the core classroom workflow if the LLM provider is unavailable?
Where and how would you enforce isolation between schools?
